title: Love the Lord Your God
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Love the Lord Your God - Pastor Tito Lyro - Deuteronomy 6_1-9.txt

outline.title: Context: Israel at the Jordan — Hearing the Covenant Renewed
subpoints: Moses reviews God's commands as Israel prepares to cross the Jordan after 40 years in the wilderness
New challenges ahead: other nations, loss of cohesion, stronger temptations to forsake the Lord
The command in Deuteronomy 6:5 is universal — not just for Israel but for every person who has ever lived
Jesus in Matthew 22 calls this the first and greatest commandment, on which all the law and prophets depend

outline.title: The Basis for the Command: Yahweh Is the Absolute God
subpoints: The Shema: 'The Lord our God, the Lord is one' (v. 4) — there is only one God, and His name is Yahweh
The Hebrew word for 'one' (echad) often refers to a unity made of parts — consistent with Trinitarian monotheism
Each person of the Trinity may rightly be called Yahweh (Acts 28; Philippians 2 and Isaiah 45)
Yahweh is not a God among other gods — He is the only God and must be followed exclusively

outline.title: The Nature of This Love: Gracious, Complete, Intelligent, and Active
subpoints: Gracious: we cannot love God on our own — God must circumcise our hearts (Deuteronomy 30:6)
Complete: all your heart, soul, mind, and strength — every other person or thing we love must be a display of our love for God
Intelligent: love for God is informed love, rooted in what He has revealed; we cannot love Him without loving His Word
Active: true love for the Lord is always evidenced by what we do — obedience, not mere feeling

outline.title: Loving God by Teaching Our Children His Word
subpoints: The first demonstration of love for God in context is teaching children diligently (vv. 6-9)
The Word is to govern every moment: sitting, walking, lying down, rising up, on the doorposts
This demands a Christian philosophy of all education — not just Bible lessons, but all learning under God's Word
Parents who love God must teach children to love God; parents who don't love the church produce children who don't love Jesus

outline.title: Living Out Our Love for God: Every Relationship Transformed
subpoints: When we live out our love for God, every relationship is affected — marriage, parenting, work, church
Husbands will love their wives as Christ loves the church; wives will honor husbands; children will obey parents
Generosity with resources becomes the norm for those who love the Lord
To decide not to use what God has given us for His glory is to put ourselves in God's place

summary:
In this exposition of Deuteronomy 6:1-9, Pastor Lyro teaches on the Shema — Israel's most recognizable prayer and the passage Jesus called the first and greatest commandment. Preached with Israel pictured standing on the eastern bank of the Jordan after forty years in the wilderness, the sermon shows that this command to love God is not for a small ethnic group in an ancient moment but is the universal standard for every person who has ever lived, affirmed by Christ Himself in Matthew 22.

The sermon identifies four characteristics of the love God commands. It is a gracious love — we cannot produce it ourselves; God must circumcise our hearts (Deuteronomy 30:6), and we love only because He first loved us (1 John 4:19). It is a complete love — with all our heart, soul, mind, and strength, meaning every skill, talent, and relationship must be subsumed under and expressed as love for God. It is an intelligent love — rooted in what God has revealed, which means we cannot love Him without loving His Word; the command to love is immediately followed by the command to teach the Word everywhere. And it is an active love — not merely a feeling, but evidenced by obedience: "we show that we love the Lord by doing what He says He wants us to do."

Pastor Lyro applies the text broadly to every sphere of life. The first application from the text itself is Christian education: the Word is to govern children's learning at every moment (sitting, walking, lying down, rising). Beyond that, living out love for God transforms every relationship: marriages, parent-child relationships, use of gifts and resources, and decisions about work and self-control. The sermon closes with a simple and urgent answer to the question "Why love God?": because as one redeemed by Christ, you cannot do anything else — if you understand what God has done for you in Jesus Christ, love is the only possible response.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading

For our Scripture reading today, we're turning to the book of Deuteronomy, chapter 6. We're going to read verses 1 through 9. This is the Word of our Lord:

"Now this is the commandment, and these are the statutes and judgments which the Lord your God has commanded to teach you, that you may observe them in the land which you are crossing over to possess, that you may fear the Lord your God, to keep all His statutes and His commandments which I command you, you and your son and your grandson, all the days of your life, and that your days may be prolonged. Therefore hear, O Israel, and be careful to observe it, that it may be well with you, and that you may multiply greatly as the Lord God of your fathers has promised you — a land flowing with milk and honey.

"Hear, O Israel: The Lord our God, the Lord is one. You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, and with all your strength. And these words which I command you today shall be in your heart. You shall teach them diligently to your children, and shall talk of them when you sit in your house, when you walk by the way, when you lie down, and when you rise up. You shall bind them as a sign on your hand, and they shall be as frontlets between your eyes. You shall write them on the doorposts of your house and on your gates."

This is the Word of our Lord — and may He write them in the tablets of our hearts. Let us pray together. Father, we pray that You bless the proclamation of Your Word, pray that Your Spirit would attend the proclamation and the hearing of it, and that You would draw us to You. In Jesus' name, amen.

## Introduction: Israel at the Jordan

When you read the book of Deuteronomy, you really should picture Israel sitting across the Jordan from Palestine. They are at the end of their journey, and Israel is about to change its life in a very significant way. For 40 years, the people of God have done everything as one. They have been sojourners in the wilderness, they have wandered, they found encouraging things in the Lord, and the Lord has disciplined them as well. Now they are about to enter the land and the rest they have been looking for for the last 40 years since leaving Egypt.

They are going to face new challenges. They are going to have to deal with other nations living among them. They are not going to be as cohesive as they are now. The temptations to forsake the Lord are going to be stronger. So Moses takes the time to review with them all that God has done for them and what He has commanded and taught them. As the people are getting ready to cross the Jordan, the Lord graciously reminds them of the covenant that He made with their fathers at Sinai.

From chapter 1 on, Moses reminds them of all that God has done for them during the years in the wilderness — how their shoes did not develop holes, how their clothes remained intact for 40 years, and how God, because He had made a covenant with them, preserved them. He tells them that the covenant He entered with their fathers was for them also. Not just for the generation that died in the wilderness, but for them — and for their children. And at the heart of this covenant is the command to love and obey the covenant God.

Six times in the book of Deuteronomy, the Lord exhorts His people to love Him. But lest you think what we read here in Deuteronomy 6 is just for Israel, I want you to notice the universality of this commandment. This is not a commandment given to a small ethnic group waiting to cross a muddy river across from Palestine 3,000 years ago. This command that we find in Deuteronomy 6:5 is for every man, woman, and child who has ever lived or will ever live. The Lord Jesus Christ tells us that — in Matthew 22:35-40: "Teacher, which is the great commandment in the law?" And He said to him, "You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind. This is the great and first commandment, and a second is like it: you shall love your neighbor as yourself. On these two commandments depend all the law and the prophets."

Lord Jesus Christ Himself calls this the first and great commandment. Everything else hangs from it.

## The Basis for the Command: Yahweh Is One

Before we get too far, Moses gives us reasons for teaching the commandments in verses 1-2: that we may do them, that we may fear the Lord, and that our days may be prolonged. And he gives reasons why we should listen and learn: that it may go well with us, that we may multiply greatly, and that we may live in the land.

And Moses also gives us the basis for the love God commands. Look at verse 4: "Hear, O Israel: The Lord our God, the Lord is one." This is the Shema — from the first word, meaning "hear." What does "the Lord is one" mean? It means there are not many gods who comprise Yahweh. He is a single God, not a pantheon. There is only one God, and His name is Yahweh. Don't worship something else.

Sometimes people have a hang-up here with the word "one." How can He be one and three persons? The Hebrew word *echad* is often used of a unity made of several parts. For example, in the tabernacle construction (Exodus 26:6), the curtains are coupled together to become "one" tabernacle — a single whole made of several parts. And all that Moses is telling us here is: the true God is Yahweh, the God of Israel; don't look for Him somewhere else.

Yahweh is the covenant name of the Godhead and may rightly be used for each person of the Trinity. The Holy Spirit may be called Yahweh — Paul in Acts 28 quotes Isaiah 6 and attributes the words of Yahweh to the Spirit. The Son, God the Son, may also be called Yahweh — in Philippians 2, every knee will bow to the Son, and Isaiah 45 says every knee will bow only to Yahweh, thereby equating them.

## The Nature of This Love

This God requires love from His people — look at verse 5: "You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, and with all your strength." Why does God require His people to love Him? Because it is the best thing they can do for themselves. There is no greater good He could require of us than to love Him. And He requires us to love Him because He first loved us.

Look at chapter 7:7-9: "The Lord did not set His love on you nor choose you because you were more in number than any other people, for you were the least of all peoples; but because the Lord loves you... the Lord has brought you out with a mighty hand." God chose Israel not because of their greatness but because of His faithfulness and love. He loved them first, and then they were able to love Him back. First John 4:19: "We love, because He first loved us."

This love God commands has four characteristics.

**First, it is a gracious love.** Left to ourselves, we cannot love God. But God Himself provides the grace to obey Him. In Deuteronomy 30:6: "The Lord your God will circumcise your heart and the heart of your descendants to love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul, that you may live." So God says, "Do all these things that display love to Me." But before you can do that, He says, "I'm going to circumcise your heart that you might love Me." The love God requires is a love based on the work of grace in our lives.

**Second, it is a complete love.** When we put all three Synoptic Gospels together — Matthew, Mark, and Luke — we hear Jesus saying: love the Lord your God with all your heart, soul, mind, and strength. Every effort, every skill, every talent, every gift, every cell in our bodies is to be involved in loving God. And the implication is that if we are to love the Lord with all our being, then every other person or thing we love must be a display of our love for God.

**Third, it is an intelligent love.** Society defines love as purposefully refusing to acknowledge that anything is wrong with anyone. Love today is described as blind acceptance. But the Scriptures tell us we are to love our God with our minds. Our love is an informed love. That is why right after telling us to love the Lord with all our being, Moses says, "Teach these things to your children." Our love for God is an informed love — which means education is critically important. We need our children to be educated in the ways of the Lord. Not just Bible lessons, but all education is to be governed by this desire to love God. We cannot love Him without loving His Word. Look at verses 6 and 8: these words shall be in your heart, bound on your hand, between your eyes, written on your doorposts and gates. The Word of God is to be everywhere in your life.

Victor Hamilton says: "Love for God, if genuine, inevitably entails obedience to the Word of God. One cannot love Him with all his heart but be lukewarm toward His Word." People of God, the plain truth is this: you can say you love God as much as you want. But if His Word means nothing to you — if you're not in it, not reading it, not studying it — your claim that you love God may not be true.

**Fourth, it is an active love.** True love for the Lord is always evidenced by what we do. What we do is not the same as loving God, but loving God is always evidenced by what we do. John says clearly in 1 John 4:20-21: "If anyone says, 'I love God,' and hates his brother, he is a liar; for he who does not love his brother whom he has seen cannot love God whom he has not seen." The second great commandment is an evidence of the presence of the first.

## Living Out Our Love for God

In context, the first demonstration of love for God is training our children to love God — verses 6-9. People of God, we get to live our love for our God. And that means believing what the Bible says concerning Him. Every time we sin, we say, "I am god and God is not." Douglas Bookman says: "God deserves to be honored, worshiped, trusted, feared, and loved as God. Our responsibility and privilege is to glorify Him — to live our lives so that all who encounter us will have a higher regard for God than they might have had they never encountered us."

When we live out our love for our God, all relationships will be affected. Husbands will love their wives with the same love that Christ loves the church. Wives will honor and respect their husbands in the Lord. Parents will not provoke their children to wrath but will raise them in the nurture and admonition of the Lord. Children will honor and obey their parents in the Lord. Older men will be sober, reverent, temperate, sound in faith. Older women will teach younger women to love their husbands and children. Young men will be self-controlled. Single people will serve the Lord wholeheartedly.

When we live out our love for the Lord, decisions about education will be affected. How we choose to use the gifts God gave us will be affected. How we choose to spend the resources God has given us will be affected — generosity with our resources becomes the norm for those who love the Lord.

And those decisions about our children's education — look at verses 7-8: you shall teach the Word diligently to your children, talk of them when you sit in your house, when you walk, when you lie down, when you rise up. There's no time when the Word of God is not to govern what your children are taught.

## Conclusion: Why Love God?

People of God, as we come to the end of this sermon, let me ask: why love God? Why love Him?

The answer is simple. As one who has been redeemed by Christ, you cannot do anything else but love God. If you know Christ — if you understand that you were dead in your trespasses and sins, that you were at enmity with God, that you were unlovable and yet God brought you out of the depths of sin and gave you a new heart and made you a new creature because of the life, death, resurrection, and ascension of Jesus Christ, that He adopted you as one of His children and is in you through His Spirit — if you understand that, if that is who you are, there is nothing you can do but love God.

So people of God: "You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, and with all your strength." May He give us the grace to do so.
